e10cb8c0-2cdb-4f2d-a377-577f09404d08.label: text_chunk
e10cb8c0-2cdb-4f2d-a377-577f09404d08.text:
arXiv:1612.05202v1  [cs.CL]  15 Dec 2016
BUILDING A ROBUST SENTIMENT LEXICON WITH (ALMOST) NO RESOURCE Mickael Rouvier Benoit Favre

Aix-Marseille University CNRS, LIF UMR 7279 13000, Marseille, France

ABSTRACT

Creating sentiment polarity lexicons is labor intensive.

85ba97c1-e656-4ca8-a22c-212edf149cca.label: text_chunk
85ba97c1-e656-4ca8-a22c-212edf149cca.text:
Automatically translating them from resourceful languages requires in-domain machine translation systems, which rely on large quantities of bi-texts. In this paper, we propose to replace machine translation by transferring words from the lexicon through word embeddings aligned across languages with a simple linear transform. The approach leads to no degradation, compared to machine translation, when tested on sentiment polarity classification on tweets from four lan- guages.

1.

618e7ad6-7906-4d36-a8d2-342c895278cd.label: text_chunk
618e7ad6-7906-4d36-a8d2-342c895278cd.text:
INTRODUCTION

Sentiment analysis is a task that aims at recognizing in text the opinion of the writer. It is often modeled as a classifi- cation problem which relies on features extracted from the text in order to feed a classifier.

6f01ae5c-5de8-4391-8891-3877fa6719d6.label: text_chunk
6f01ae5c-5de8-4391-8891-3877fa6719d6.text:
Relevant features proposed in the literature span from microblogging artifacts including hashtags, emoticons [1, 2], intensifiers like all-caps words and character repetitions [3], sentiment-topic features [4], to the inclusion of polarity lexicons.

The objective of the work presented in this paper is the creation of sentiment polarity lexicons. They are word lists or phrase lists with positive and negative sentiment labels.

81164a10-4b23-4c9d-8ca6-2eff2cfc3dde.label: text_chunk
81164a10-4b23-4c9d-8ca6-2eff2cfc3dde.text: Sen- timent lexicons allow to increase the feature space with more relevant and generalizing characteristics of the input. Unfor- tunately, creating sentiment lexicons requires human exper- tise, is time consuming, and often results in limited coverage when dealing with new domains.

178c96cc-d287-4c32-b844-48b18e64f424.label: text_chunk
178c96cc-d287-4c32-b844-48b18e64f424.text: In the literature, it has been proposed to extend existing lexicons without supervision [5, 6], or to automatically trans- late existing lexicons from resourceful languages with statisti- cal machine translation (SMT) systems [7]. While the former requires seed lexicons, the later are very interesting because they can automate the process of generating sentiment lexi- cons without any human expertise.

bac80802-dc3c-4893-9341-ac8ef7fc0062.label: text_chunk
bac80802-dc3c-4893-9341-ac8ef7fc0062.text: But automatically trans- lating sentiment lexicons leads to two problems: (1) out-of- vocabulary words, such as mis-spellings, morphological vari- ants and slang, cannot be translated, and (2) machine trans- lation performance strongly depends on available training re- sources such as bi-texts.

0c998c89-62de-40f5-8479-13c31104cbb2.label: text_chunk
0c998c89-62de-40f5-8479-13c31104cbb2.text: In this paper, we propose to apply the method proposed in [8] for automatically mapping word embeddings across languages and use them to translate sentiment lexicons only given a small, general bilingual dictionary. After creating monolingual word embeddings in the source and target lan- guage, we train a linear transform on the bilingual dictionary and apply that transform to words for which we don’t have a translation.

ae5166ed-8042-4f46-9bcd-ed3a83edf142.label: text_chunk
ae5166ed-8042-4f46-9bcd-ed3a83edf142.text: We perform experiments on 3-class polarity classifica- tion in tweets, and report results on four different languages: French, Italian, Spanish and German. Existing English senti- ment lexicons are translated to the target languages through the proposed approach, given gs trained on the respective Wikipedia of each language. Then, a SVM-based classifier is fed with lexicon features, comparing machine translation with embedding transfer.

90c28991-14d7-48d3-9300-bb42138c6bac.label: text_chunk
90c28991-14d7-48d3-9300-bb42138c6bac.text:
After presenting related work in Section 2, the extraction of word gs and their mapping across languages are detailed in Section 3. The corpus on which experiments are carried out and the results of our experiments are presented in Section 4. Finally, we conclude with a discussion of possible directions in Section 5.

2.

e77e425e-fda7-46dd-8c67-bcabb3eceadc.label: text_chunk
e77e425e-fda7-46dd-8c67-bcabb3eceadc.text:
RELATED WORK

Many methods have been proposed for extending polarity lex- icons: propagate polarity along thesaurus relations [9, 10, 11] or use cooccurrence statistics to identify similar words [12, 13].

dea5de29-93d2-4fc7-986c-64c10d4f7209.label: text_chunk
dea5de29-93d2-4fc7-986c-64c10d4f7209.text:
Porting lexicons to other languages has also been studied: use aligned thesauri and propagate at the sense level [14, 15], translate the lexicon directly [16, 17], take advantage of off- the-shelf translation and include sample word context to get better translations [18] or use crowd sourcing to quickly boot- strap lexicons in non-english languages [19].

3.

6186e94b-ebf5-405c-9513-14fafc65bd6d.label: text_chunk
6186e94b-ebf5-405c-9513-14fafc65bd6d.text:
APPROACH

Our approach consists in creating distributional word repre- sentations in the source and target languages, and map them to each other with a linear transform trained given a small
bilingual dictionary of frequent words. Then, source language words from the polarity lexicon can be projected in the target language embedding.

d6b01f21-51ae-415f-9f01-19ac2f7a11f3.label: text_chunk
d6b01f21-51ae-415f-9f01-19ac2f7a11f3.text:
The closest words to the projecting are used as translation.In our experiments, word embeddings are estimated on the source and target language Wikipedia corpora using the word2vec toolkit [20].

 The embeddings are trained using skip-gram approach with a window of size 7 and 5 iterations. The dimension of the embeddings is fixed to 200.[21] have shown that the skip-gram word embedding model is in fact a linear decomposition of the cooccurrence matrix.

0949ad6e-8855-409e-b739-ad62ae0b101e.label: text_chunk
0949ad6e-8855-409e-b739-ad62ae0b101e.text:
This decomposition is unique up to a linear transfor- mation.

 Therefore, given two word representations created from the same cooccurrence matrix, a linear transform can be devised to map words from the first to the second.

3bf35c38-dfbe-4a5e-8a26-75f52fb9ef59.label: text_chunk
3bf35c38-dfbe-4a5e-8a26-75f52fb9ef59.text:
As- suming that cooccurrence matrices for the source and target languages are sampled from the same language-independent cooccurrent matrix, one can find a linear transform for map- ping source words to target words, up to an error component which represents sampling error.

 This assumption is realis- tic for comparable corpora, such as embeddings trained on wikipedia in various languages.

72f28b2f-799a-4e8b-bb25-f1793ece02b0.label: text_chunk
72f28b2f-799a-4e8b-bb25-f1793ece02b0.text:
In our experiments, we pre- ferred to estimate word embeddings on Wikipedia rather than Twitter corpora because across languages, Tweets can cover different event from different countries, reducing the overlap.However, word embeddings represent a mixture from the senses of each word, making the cross-language mapping non bijective (a word can have multiple translations), which will probably contribute to the residual.

 Therefore, it should be reasonable to train a linear transform to map words between the source and target languages.

388d68b4-4d5c-47b2-be7d-69f9297ab9ad.label: text_chunk
388d68b4-4d5c-47b2-be7d-69f9297ab9ad.text: Note that a linear transform would conserve the translations associated to linguistic regu- larities observed in the vector spaces.The idea is to translate words in another language in the goal to generate sentiment lexicon.

836b3934-9b9c-41a6-9d9a-7215d9c30f09.label: text_chunk
836b3934-9b9c-41a6-9d9a-7215d9c30f09.text: In [8], the authors propose to estimate a transformation matrix W such that W x = y , where x is the embedding of a word in the source language and y is the embedding of its translation in the target lan- guage.In order to estimate the W matrix, suppose we are given a set of word pairs and their associated vector representations { x i , y i } where x i is the embeddings of word i in the source language and y i is the embedding of its translation.

de637341-692f-47f6-927a-cc8eb7ffef4d.label: text_chunk
de637341-692f-47f6-927a-cc8eb7ffef4d.text:
The ma- trix W can be learned by the following optimization problem:
min W X i || W x i − y i || 2 (1)
which we solve with the least square method.At prediction time, for any given new word x , we can map it to the other language space by computing y = W x . Then we find the words whose representations are closest to y in the target language space using the cosine similarity as distance metric.

9d6b1265-69ca-47e1-ab28-4b7cf25ffd38.label: text_chunk
9d6b1265-69ca-47e1-ab28-4b7cf25ffd38.text:
In our experiments, we select all representa- tions which cosine similarity is superior to λ (with λ = 0 . 65
set empirically).In practice, we only have manual translations for a small subset of words, not necessarily polarity infused, on which we train W . We use that W to find translations for all words of the sentiment lexicon.

4. EXPERIMENTS

4.1.

817a64e8-5e4a-4ce2-b665-632f9c9beb32.label: text_chunk
817a64e8-5e4a-4ce2-b665-632f9c9beb32.text:
Corpus and Metrics

The sentiment polarity classification task is set as a three-class problem: positive, negative and neutral. The metrics used to measure performance is macro-fmeasure. We developed our system on French and apply the same components on Italian, Spanish and German. A concise description of the training data follows.The French (FR) corpus comes from the DEFT’15 evalu- ation campaign 1 .

0e1cd8ee-83d6-4226-b164-0a0f00bfc211.label: text_chunk
0e1cd8ee-83d6-4226-b164-0a0f00bfc211.text:
It consists of 7,836 tweets for training and 3,381 tweets for testing.

 The Italian (IT) corpus was released as part of the SentiPOLC’14 evaluation campaign [25]. It consists of 4,513 tweets for training and 1,930 tweets for test- ing. For Spanish (ES), the TASS’15 corpus is used [26].

93f3b0f3-6bf5-43d3-8256-f45cfc4a0362.label: text_chunk
93f3b0f3-6bf5-43d3-8256-f45cfc4a0362.text:
Since the evaluation campaign was still ongoing at the time of writ- ing, we use 3-fold validation on the training corpus composed of 7,219 tweets.

 German (DE) tweets come from the Multi- lingual Sentiment Dataset [27]. It consists of 844 tweets for training and 844 tweets for testing.In order to extract features on those corpora, polarity lex- icons are translated from English using the method described in Section 3.

a6c0f05a-7359-4f54-8f24-73da231a6ee6.label: text_chunk
a6c0f05a-7359-4f54-8f24-73da231a6ee6.text:
The following lexicons are translated:

• MPQA :

 The MPQA (Multi-Perspective Question An- swering) lexicon is composed of 4913 negatives words and 2718 positives words [28].

• BingLiu :

 This lexicon contains 2006 positive words and 4783 negative words. This lexicon includes mis- spellings, morphological variants and slang [29].

164bc885-e9d4-4844-9174-40d6072b166c.label: text_chunk
164bc885-e9d4-4844-9174-40d6072b166c.text:
• HGI : The Harvard General Inquirer (HGI) lexicons contains several dictionaries,

 we only used positive and negative lexicons that contains respectively 1915 and 2291 words [30].

• NRC :

 NRC Emotion Lexicon is a large word list con- structed by Amazon Mechanical Turk [31].

4.2. System

In order to test the value of the create lexicons, we use them in a typical sentiment polarity classification system [32].

26dc434a-55f9-4184-9e06-25bd7eb16cf3.label: text_chunk
26dc434a-55f9-4184-9e06-25bd7eb16cf3.text:
We first tokenize the tweets with a tokenizer based on macaon [33]. Then, hashtags and usertags are mapped to generic tokens. Each tweet is represented with the following

66eb137a-564e-49de-a478-810e5f64f568.label: text_chunk
66eb137a-564e-49de-a478-810e5f64f568.text: Results in macro-fmeasure obtained on the different languages (French, Italian, Spanish and German) using different sentiment lexicon (MPQA, BingLiu, HGI and NRC).features and an SVM classifier with a linear kernel is trained to perform the task.

0c6dc85c-d6e1-4c44-83e5-86a7ee366e71.label: text_chunk
0c6dc85c-d6e1-4c44-83e5-86a7ee366e71.text:
• Words n-grams

• All-caps: the number of words with all characters in upper case

• Hashtags: the number of hashtags

• Lexicons: number of words present in each lexicon

• Punctuation: the number of contiguous sequences of exclamation marks, question marks,

13edd5cb-3ce3-4682-ac09-ced850a31b2d.label: text_chunk
13edd5cb-3ce3-4682-ac09-ced850a31b2d.text:
and both exclama- tion and question marks

• Last punctuation: whether the last token contains an exclamation or question mark

• Emoticons:

 presence or absence of positive and nega- tive emoticons at any position in the tweet

• Last emoticon: whether the last token is a positive or negative emoticon

• Elongated words:

 the number of words with one char- acter repeated more than three times,

4691e4d6-8007-4803-bd4e-3935a0159040.label: text_chunk
4691e4d6-8007-4803-bd4e-3935a0159040.text:
for example : “loooool”
We did not implement part-of-speech and cluster features as they cannot be assumed to be available in the target lan- guages. This system was part of the system combination that obtained the best results at the TASS 2015 [26, 34] and DEFT 2015 [35, 36] evaluation campaigns.

4.3. Results

Table 1 reports the results of the system and different base- lines.

100de882-f3a3-444d-b1e9-82c53aae956b.label: text_chunk
100de882-f3a3-444d-b1e9-82c53aae956b.text: The No Sentiment Lexicon system does not have any lexicon feature. It obtains a macro-fmeasure of 60.65 on the four corpora.Systems denoted [22], [23], [24] are baselines that cor- respond respectively to unsupervised, supervised and semi- supervised approaches for generating the lexicon. We observe that adding sentiment lexicons improves performance.

8ba07a41-97a8-48c3-818d-d07e7acc9ce6.label: text_chunk
8ba07a41-97a8-48c3-818d-d07e7acc9ce6.text: The Moses system consists in translating the different sen- timent lexicons with the Moses SMT toolkit. It is trained on the Europarl bi-texts. The approach based on translation ob- tains better results than the Baseline systems. In our exper- iments, we observe that some words have not been correctly translated (for example: slang words).

4231bb20-a625-46a5-a17f-fa9456a0a112.label: text_chunk
4231bb20-a625-46a5-a17f-fa9456a0a112.text: The main drawback on this approach is that for correctly translating sentiment lexica, the SMT system must be trained on in-domain bi-texts..Fig. 1 . Average macro-fmeasure over the four languages when training the linear transform with a bilingual dictionary of n most frequent words (MPQA sentiment lexicon).The BWE (Bilingual Word Embeddings) system consists in translating the sentiment lexicons with our method. This approach obtains results comparable to the SMT approach.

b5368652-cb24-4a12-95f2-fd3f4c10e7b4.label: text_chunk
b5368652-cb24-4a12-95f2-fd3f4c10e7b4.text: The main advantage of this approach is to be able to general- ize on words unknown to the SMT system.Moses and BWE can be combined by creating a lexicon from the union of the lexicons obtained by those systems. This combination yields even better results than translation or mapping alone.Our second experiment consists in varying the size of the bilingual dictionary used to train W .

5a53beef-2e36-4f4d-8995-8170c1da62d4.label: text_chunk
5a53beef-2e36-4f4d-8995-8170c1da62d4.text: Figure 1 shows the evo- lution of average macro f-measure (over the four languages) when the n most frequent words from Wikipedia are part of the bilingual dictionary. It can be observed that using the 50k most frequent words leads to the best performance (an aver- age macro-fmeasure of 61.72) while only 1,000 words already brings nice improvements.Fig. 2 .

bd453460-c65a-4847-8bf0-4559f275bb5c.label: text_chunk
bd453460-c65a-4847-8bf0-4559f275bb5c.text: Average macro-fmeasure over the four language when training the linear transform with a small part of the lexicon words (MPQA sentiment lexicon).In a last experiment, we look into the gains that can be obtained by manually translating a small part of the lexicon and use it as bilingual dictionary when training the transfor- mation matrix.

6f6a67f1-9062-4b06-9d26-1047bb974494.label: text_chunk
6f6a67f1-9062-4b06-9d26-1047bb974494.text:
Figure 2 shows average macro-fmeasure on the four languages when translating up to 2,000 words from the MPQA lexicon (out of 8k). It can be observed that from 600 words on, performance is better than that of the statistical translation system.

5. CONCLUSIONS

This paper is focused on translating sentiment polarity lexi- cons from a resourceful language through word embeddings mapped from the source to the target language.

cb1c360e-741a-42f5-ac90-9eb6552ceb9e.label: text_chunk
cb1c360e-741a-42f5-ac90-9eb6552ceb9e.text:
Experiments on four languages with mappings from English show that the approach performs as well as full-fledged SMT. While the ap- proach was successful for languages close to English where word-to-word translations are possible, it may not be as effec- tive for languages where this assumption does not hold.

 We will explore this aspect for future work.

**Summary of Relationships Involving MagiCoder**

1. **Testing and Evaluation**: MagiCoder has been rigorously tested on a dataset of approximately 4500 manually revised reports to assess its performance against human revisions. Its effectiveness is further evaluated using benchmarks and performance metrics such as PT, which measures error minimization and recall precision.

2. **Functionality and Purpose**: Designed to assist pharmacologists and pharmacovigilance stakeholders, MagiCoder streamlines the encoding of Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) reports, significantly reducing the time required for this task. It processes narrative descriptions of suspected ADRs and generates relevant MedDRA terms, enhancing the efficiency of report revisions.

3. **Technological Integration**: MagiCoder utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms to support pharmacovigilance activities and is implemented as a plug-in for VigiFarmaco, extending its functionality. It maps Italian text to the MedDRA dictionary and recognizes terms within the LLT (Lowest Level Terms) framework.

4. **Collaboration and Development**: The software is developed in collaboration with pharmacovigilance experts and is part of a set of voted solutions, indicating its relevance in the coding community. It also collaborates with human experts to improve the coding of adverse events.

5. **User Interaction**: MagiCoder is designed for direct interaction with users, requiring quick responses to queries. It assists human experts by providing tools to identify redundancy errors, thereby enhancing decision-making in data encoding.

6. **Performance and Limitations**: The performance of MagiCoder is influenced by the complexities of the Italian language, particularly due to synonymical groups that affect coding accuracy. It contrasts with part-of-speech methodologies, as it does not consider sentence structure in its analysis.

7. **Future Enhancements**: Suggestions for improvement include integrating a spell checker feature from VigiFarmaco to enhance user input accuracy and reduce errors. Additionally, MagiCoder aims to test its procedures against various medical classification systems, including ICD-9.

Overall, MagiCoder represents a significant advancement in the automation of pharmacovigilance processes, enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of ADR reporting and coding through advanced technology and collaborative development.
